abstract: This paper examines the load-bearing capacity of steel railway bridges constructed during the second half of the 19th century, serving as a critical discussion of findings recently published in the journal "Roads and Bridges - Drogi i Mosty" (No. 2, 2014). The analysis evaluates the structural integrity of these historical assets in light of modern Eurocode standards, specifically addressing a bridge dismantled in 2007 after 122 years of service. A central point of contention in current engineering assessments is the appropriate yield strength value to assume for aged steel; while laboratory tests often yield higher averages, standardized safety protocols may necessitate more conservative estimates. It is concluded that any accurate verification of the load-carrying capacity of long-exploited bridges must go beyond initial delivery parameters and account for the long-term effects of spontaneous aging on material properties. Furthermore, the discussion emphasizes that while modern safety criteria are rigorous, their application to historical structures requires a balanced approach to avoid unnecessary demolitions of viable infrastructure.
